metadata.name: Global Semiconductor Shortage
metadata.source: SIA / Gartner / IHS Markit / TSMC Annual Reports
metadata.description: A confluence of COVID-19 demand shifts, work-from-home electronics surges, automotive chip cancellations-then-panic-buying, and fab capacity constraints triggered the most severe global semiconductor shortage in decades. TSMC, holding roughly 54% of the global foundry market, became the central bottleneck. The automotive industry alone lost an estimated $500B in revenue over the crisis, with lead times stretching from a normal 12 weeks to over 52 weeks for some components.

recovery_milestones.description: Automakers cancel chip orders during COVID lockdowns
Consumer electronics demand surges; fab capacity reallocated
Auto industry attempts to re-order; discovers 6-month backlogs
Global shortage acknowledged; governments begin CHIPS Act discussions
New fab investments announced (TSMC Arizona, Intel Ohio, Samsung Texas)
Legacy node shortages begin easing; advanced nodes remain constrained
Broad normalization of supply; inventory levels recovering

lessons_learned: Extreme market concentration (TSMC at 54% foundry share) creates systemic single-point-of-failure risk for the entire global economy.
Just-in-time ordering with zero buffer inventory converts a moderate demand shift into a catastrophic shortage via the bullwhip effect.
Automotive OEMs discovered they had no direct relationships with foundries and were de-prioritized behind higher-margin consumer electronics customers.
Long fab construction cycles (2-4 years) mean capacity cannot respond to demand shocks in real time; strategic reserves and dual-sourcing are essential.
Government intervention (US CHIPS Act, EU Chips Act) reflected recognition that semiconductor supply is a national security concern.
